Welcome to the Gary Hilbert Letters All-Star Audio Series. I'm Kevin Rogers. In this episode you'll hear direct marketing legend Dan Kennedy read Gary's classic newsletter all about grabbers. Bonded Kevin chose Dan to read this newsletter, one of Gary's most popular, because he's not only an old school marketer who still uses grabbers very effectively to this day. He's also an old school guy who refuses to use the internet and conducts all communication with prospects, clients, and colleagues through the fax machine. This refusal to do things anyway but his own is just one of the reasons why Dan Kennedy continues to excel at profiting for both his own products and those of his clients, who by the way, pay him upwards of $100,000 to write their direct mail campaigns. I'll share some hilarious personal stories about Gary in this exclusive recording and even share some fresh advice for marketers of any style and generation so be sure to listen all the way through. Here now is Dan Kennedy with Grabbers. Hi, this is Dan Kennedy. In a few minutes I'll be reading aloud an issue of Gary Hilbert's newsletter where he pontificates on the use of grabbers, objects, and freemiums in direct mail. And I'll have a few comments of my own on the subject. First though, my Hilbert story. I knew of Gary long before I knew Gary. Many of the kids I went to high school with had after school jobs working for Hilbert's when it was in full swing selling the family crests, with a sales letter discussed in the issue of his newsletter I'm about to read. Hilbert's strange business and the fact of thousands of envelopes needing open and checks and cash removed from them was well known and gossiped about. While still in high school, I got my first fee for copywriting from Hilbert's, although I did so without encountering Gary. As I recall, I was paid $500 for a beat the control success with a sales letter for beer mugs. I said authentic Austrian beer mugs, with your family crest engraved on them. I recall having a sentence about old world craftsmanship, old spelled O-L-D-E stricken, because of course the mugs were mass engraved by machine, with every Kennedy getting the same Kennedy crest, every Düsseldorf getting the same Düsseldorf crest. I was robbed by the way, but at the time getting paid $500 for anything was so amazing to me I felt like the thief. It was years later that I got a sales letter from Gary for his newsletter, the one with the photo of Ernest Borg dying on it. Gary was gone from Barberton, Ohio in Florida then, and I was gone from Bath, Ohio and in Phoenix. On impulse I picked up the phone called and got him on the phone and a quick, we're from the same neighborhood conversation turned into an hour or so and an immediate invitation to come to Key West the next weekend and speak at and be a panelist at his big $7000 per person seminar. I have interesting, instructive and classic Halbert stories about my work at every one of those seminars, but there's no time for all that here. Gary and I became friends. We did a couple things together that made money, a couple things that didn't. I wrote a sales letter that mailed just once just to his list that brought in over $200,000 when $200,000 was a respectable sum of money. I inherited a few clients orphaned by or disenchanted with him, which always called and spoke with him about before accepting. We had a bit of friendly rivalry. Every once in a while he poked me in the ribs with a snarky reference in his newsletter. Every once in a while he mentioned me favorably. As a last piece of trivia, when my first wife and I divorced and she returned to Ohio from Arizona, she went job seeking and went to an interview at Numa Limited, the company born from the original Halberts. She kept Kennedy as her last name and Gary's ex-partner Dennis Haslinger recognized the name instantly, determined she'd been Dan Kennedy trained and made her his executive assistant on the spot. I'd met and had a couple meals with Dennis once in Key West. After this, while she was there, he thanked me for providing such an extraordinary assistant. I thanked him for making alimony unnecessary. As my business life has unfolded, I have had synergistic yet separate and simultaneous careers as a copywriter, consultant, speaker, author, and newsletter publisher. A rather large membership organization has been built with me and my newsletters as its centerpiece, now called GKIC, which can be seen at DanKennedy.com. I have personally benefited and benefit from Halbert Jems about copywriting, about the copywriting business, and about life, and I reference Gary in my books, newsletters, and recordings. His close associate, John Carleton and I are friends and have worked together. So in various ways, Gary Halbert has been a part of my life across some 42 years and counting. Every once in a while, it is still a surprising thought that there will never again be the call out of the blue, sometimes very late at night from a bored or frustrated Halbert, or the Halbert with a tale of a preposterous misadventure that turns into a long meaning of life conversation. I again live most of the time in Ohio and occasionally drive past the Halbert landmark here or there. As a matter of fact, the studio I am recording this in is in beautiful downtown Bath, Ohio, not far from a bank branch in which Halbert rented space upstairs and about 10 minutes up a country road from the valley where Numa had its offices. Since Gary and I share love of long copy, describing him in few words seems inappropriate, but nonetheless I would class Halbert first and foremost as an iconic class. In my own relationship with him, he was also a most generous man. As a teacher, he became lazily dogmatic, but I find myself doing that too. To this subject, he certainly knew as much as anybody and more than most about crafting and mailing sales letters that went out and captured money. He was also intuitive about it and brought an intangible factor to it in the same way that Michael Jordan brought intangibles to his game. You can discern what Halbert did with a piece of copy, but that doesn't mean you can do it yourself. I once won a $500 wager in a mastermind group, the only one of 20 very smart marketers who picked the winner out of four different newspaper ads presented for consideration. I admitted that I couldn't tell anybody why the winner had outperformed all the others, and I even shared the other people's preference for a different ad, but I recognized Halbert's hand in the ad I placed my bet on and was therefore confident it had beaten the other three even though I could not explain or defend it as the better of the ads. As a copywriter, I am routinely paid upwards of $100,000 plus royalties, that plus royalties thing thanks to an offhand remark by Halbert, for multimedia, multi-step campaigns, and I've been at that level for about a decade, with 85% of all clients repeating, so I'm pretty good, but I often say that while he was alive, pound for pound, dollar for dollar, when you could get him to work, Halbert was far superior and was probably the best copywriter, especially when writing to appeal to Joe Everyman. Much of Halbert is timeless, as is this newsletter Bond Halbert assigned to me to read aloud. Unlike other media, direct mail is itself a stable and steady environment where there are evergreen strategies and wherever green success is possible. I can show you a direct mail package I created 20 years ago that generated millions of dollars and one from a few months ago that has also generated millions of dollars, lay them side by side, and you can see they are near clones in architecture, even in copy. Google can't change these algorithms. What's in this issue of Gary's newsletter is in this category of perpetual effectiveness. I'm not sure why or how this particular issue was chosen for me. In the micro, it's a good pick, because I've probably mailed as many different direct mail pieces with different grabbers as anybody, and I'll mention some a little later. In the back row, I'm a very big fan of perpetual effectiveness. I like the reliable. It seems to me much more valuable to learn, understand, and own something that is perpetually effective than something with very short shelf life and very subject to disruption or change. Income can be had from the latter, but almost all wealth comes from the former. So we begin from Gary Halbert, way west of Jewfish Creek. Please remove the stress control biofeedback card from the top of this letter and hold your thumb on the dark rectangle for a count of 10. Now remove your thumb and check the color in the rectangle to find out how stressed you are at this very moment. How'd you do? Are you calm, normal, or tense? Or worst of all, did you happen to get a black reading that suggests your frame of mind is about the same as a long-tailed cat in a room full of rocking chairs? No matter. At least it's no matter if any of your stress is caused because you can't figure out how to make your direct mail more effective. You see, if you are stressed out for that reason, I want you to take that test again after you finish this letter because by then your stress level should be much lower. Why? The answer is simple. You see, you are about to learn one of my main secrets for pushing direct mail response to the outer limits of profitability by the intelligent use of what I call grabbers. What's a grabber? Hey, I'm glad you asked. Inquiring minds really do want to know, don't they? Okay, buckwheat, a grabber is what I call something you can attach to the top of a letter that grabs your reader's immediate attention and he can then grab and remove it from the letter and hold it in his grubby little hands. In other words, a grabber is something that grabs a reader's attention and then turns him into a grabby who grabs the grabber if your copy can weave an intelligent and compelling reason for doing so as I just did with you. Oh hell, you know what I mean. Now listen up. Providing you've been using all the other techniques I've been revealing to you, like A-pile, B-pile, the magic sealed envelope, etc. There is nothing I know of that can hype your response as much as a grabber. How much profit can a grabber grab? I'm going to answer that question in just a moment right after I take another of my famous little side trips to give you a useful little nugget of information. Harken to me. Listen, a short time ago I ordered something published by the Dartnell Corporation called the Greatest Direct Mail Sales Letters of All Time, how they were created, why they succeeded, and how you can create great sales letters to buy this book. I forget how much it is, but it's certainly not overpriced like all my stuff. And it's great. It truly is a collection of absolutely superb direct mail letters. Plus it has tips from the expert, a collection of letter leads, and another one letter closes. This was all put together by a guy named Richard S. Hodgson, who has done one hell of a good job, my kudos. However, after having said all that, I must also say it seems to me that Mr. Hodgson seems to have inexplicably left out from his excellent compilation what are, perhaps, the two most successful letters in all of direct mail history. The first one was written by yours truly, and it is the one pager used to sell the family name, Code of Arms Research Report, sold by Halbert's Inc. The reason I think Mr. Hodgson should have included this letter is twofold. One, it is, I believe, the most widely mailed letter in history, 140 million, all sent first class, and it produced a huge front-end profit and generated 7,300,000 cash with order customers. And the second reason is that by leaving this letter out, Mr. Hodgson has inadvertently hurt my feelings, a situation I am sure any sensitive, truly caring person would be eager to correct. Oh well, let me wipe the tears from my eyes so I can continue. More to the point of this month's subject, the second letter Mr. Hodgson left out is the most successful grabber of all time. You'll find it on page 394 in the chapter on showmanship of the Robert Collier Letter Book. Here is what it says, Dear Mr. Jones, here's a dollar, yes it's a real dollar, nice and clean to do, keep it if you want to after you've read this letter, but I don't believe you will then. Here's what it's all about, I've made an investment of a thousand dollars in human nature, human kindness, I've mailed a thousand dollars in a thousand letters to a thousand people picked at random. I've done this because I believe that everyone is really kind and that no one is really heartless and that the only reason why folks do not help where help is needed is just because those needs are not impressed upon them hard enough. And that's the mission of each of my thousand dollars, to impress the importance of a need, this thousand dollars is my subscription to the blank hospital and I'm investing it in the belief that everyone will bring back several more, at least another with it. So our subscription, which I'm starting in this way, will be at least two thousand, maybe five thousand, for there's going to be a lot of you, so send a five or a ten or more when you mail my dollar back. Remember, both my dollar and your dollars go to help crippled children. Will everyone come back? Are people really kind or really heartless? Sincerely? And how much loot did this grabber letter grab? Here's the scoop right from the book. That letter pulled better than a 90% response. The writer of it told me that from 175,000 letters mailed, he got back about 270,000 dollars, plus more than 90% of the dollar bills he had mailed out. But this was only the start. From the list of more than 150,000 people who gave that 270,000 dollars, further subscriptions were secured to the amount of some 10 million dollars. And don't forget, we're talking about 10 million dollars back when it was worth something, because this letter was mailed more than 50 years ago. And guess what else? This same letter was recently tested with modernized wording and once again got 90% results. Maybe we should look into this idea of attaching grabbers to letters a little closer, you think? Now as you probably have already guessed, my favorite type of grabber to attach to a letter is money. I use dollar bills, two dollar bills, Japanese yens, Mexican pesos, or whatever else makes sense. If it's a penny, the copy often starts like this. Dear name, as you can see, I have attached a bright shiny penny to the top of this letter. Why have I done this? Actually, there are two reasons. Number one, first, since I have something extremely important to tell you, I needed some way to make sure this letter would catch your attention. Number two, and secondly, since this letter concerns how you can make or save a lot of money, I thought using a penny as a little financial eye catcher was especially appropriate. Here's what it's all about. In the case of the Japanese yen, a very lightweight little aluminum coin, the second reason can be something like, and since what I'm writing about was discovered in Japan, or in the case of the Mexican peso, you start writing about how this once proud currency used to be the standard against how other currencies were measured, but now it takes a wheelbarrow full of pesos to buy just one cruggering. And then of course, you're going to weave into your letter a story about the ravages of inflation and how it can really happen here. And of course, why if you buy the financial report or newsletter or whatever the sales letter is touting, you won't have to worry about such a catastrophe. You know, once I did a letter from my friend, the late Joe Carbo, that sold a system to beat the horses, and I attached a $2 bill to the top of each letter, which said in part, and since I'm so sure this betting system will work for you, I've even decided to send you the attached two bucks with which to make your first bet and so on. Enough about money. Now let's see what else we can use for a grabber that has an almost universal application. Well, if you want to know about something else, which nearly always works, try sending what is or appears to be a genuine photograph. Listen, you want to be a marketing hero to yourself, your company or your clients, try this. If your direct mail sales piece currently includes a brochure, eliminate it from the next mailing and instead send what appears to be a homemade amateur Polaroid or 35 millimeter photograph. Perhaps you are sending this photo because this is a brand new product and you wanted the reader to be among the first to know about it and you didn't want to delay getting the scoop to him until you had brochures printed up. Maybe the photo is a view or whomever else is signing the letter because as you explain in your letter, you're an old fashioned guy who believes people ought to at least be able to know what a person looks like if they are going to do business with him. Maybe the photo is of a special part of the product you are selling that helps you explain and helps your reader visualize why your product works so much better than the products of your competitors. Whatever, the main thing is that by sending a photo, a very personal, very human, very warm photo, instead of a printed brochure, a very impersonal, cold and technical brochure, your letter will be humanized and that my friend is what Catapult's direct mail response to levels virtually undreamed of by most experts. What another idea you do? You're a greedy little mind sucker, aren't you? Well, good for you. Let's see, what could you and I use for a grabber if we were truly to sell beachfront condos in Maui? Hmm, how about attaching a little plastic baggie filled with sand and sending out letters which start like this? Dear name, see that little baggie stapled to the top of this letter? You want to know what's in it? Well, let me tell you, the contents of that baggie is something that's rapidly becoming one of the rarest and most valuable substances on earth. In fact, it is earth or rather sand, sand from the most exclusive beachfront property in Maui, which is rapidly disappearing, etc., etc. Zowie, I'm truly on a roll, ain't I? Let's see, what kind of grabber could we use if our brain energy is at an extremely low ebb? A condition very common to advertising people, and we can't think of anything especially applicable, but we want to use a grabber anyway. Hey, I know, how about sending a real live first-class postage stamp? No, no, not the reply envelope. I'm talking about attaching the stamp to the top of the letter, so then you can write something like this. Dear name, as you can see, I've attached a 22 cent first-class stamp to the top of this letter. Why? The answer is simple. You see, I've got a proposition for you, and I need an answer right away. So please, as soon as you finish this letter, remove the stamp and put it on the enclosed reply envelope and mail me your answer immediately. Here's why it is so important, blah, blah, blah, etc., etc. Use your imagination. Send a couple of aspirins, because the first part of this letter is going to give you a headache, and then later, your letter will present another solution for the headache, something more permanent than aspirin. Send a feather. Can you imagine having to use this instead of a word processor to create your next business report? Send a little lump of coal in a baggie. Ever see a diamond before it was old enough to sparkle? Send a, oh nuts, if I haven't gotten your mind and gear by now, you're hopeless. But you're not hopeless, are you? No, sir, you're one of my subscribers, who are truly among the smartest people in the world, like for instance, Don Clifton, a realtor in Spokane, Washington, who sends a letter with a $1 check attached that has several survey questions printed on the back, questions which help him qualify people as to whether or not they could use a speaker to inspire and inform people who work as real estate agents. The way it works is, you take off your answers on the little squares next to the questions on the back of the check, and then you deposit the check. And then when the cancel check comes back to Don with his bank statement, he'll know some useful stuff about you, and when he contacts you again, you'll damn sure remember him, won't you? So far, he's got 538 back out of less than 1500 mail, and each of his check statements brings more hot leads. Isn't that a neat idea? Of course, in the next few months or so, I'll get real creative and maybe figure some way I can take credit for this little gem. Why not? I've always suspected I might be missing some thrills by being an original instead of a thief. Sincerely, Gary C. Halbert. That's it for this month. Except for one admonition. Don't miss next month's letter. When you get it, read it immediately, because it will be, by far, the most important one I've ever written. Peace. And that ends Sir Gary's letter about this. I imagine that I and my clients have collectively spent at least $25 million on grabbers, objects, and freebubes, so I have a few thoughts to add. I'll start with a few general comments. What Gary understood all too well, driving his love of grabbers, is that you can never presume readership, and you must have it to get a chance at winning. A lot of people are stupid arrogant about this. They assume that because they've mailed something, the recipients will read it. Many even feel entitled to readership. What I know is that getting someone to invest time and attention in my message is something I must creatively earn and often need to buy. Marketers are also too often stupid cheap about this. Gee, sending a whole bag of Kellogg's cornflakes is bulky and expensive. Can't we just send a photo of a box of cornflakes? You have to know what it is worth to you to get what you send, actually, read or read and listen to or read and watched. If you don't know your numbers, you can't make smart decisions about any of this. Gary loved and I've long loved and loved direct mail for many good reasons and a few emotional biased ones. We are print and direct mail guys. Today I use direct mail a lot to drive prospects to online media and presentations, but the game is still all about the direct mail. He and I admire great direct mail, like some sophisticated folks like Michael Masterson might admire fine art or ordinary guys ogle hot sexy women. One of the reasons I prefer direct mail, delivered as mail in priority mail boxes or tubes or FedEx or UPS is the ability to enclose attention, getting and attention, obligating objects. Now, as Gary would say, harken unto me, buckaroos, because I'm about to teach you something worth millions. There are two reasons, not just one, to use objects and grabbers attention, getting and attention obligating. Those are two different purposes that may be accommodated by the same or by multiple objects or items, but the latter impact is not understood by a lot of marketers. Getting attention momentarily is one thing. My friend and colleague Sally Hogsett, author of the great book Fascinate has documented the decline of attention span to that of a goldfish. Nine seconds, nine seconds. If you deliver something a person thinks is cool or funny or useful that he will keep, keep on a shelf, show around the office, take home for his kid or eat, you buy a certain sense of obligation from reasonably honest and ethical folks. They'll feel badly about keeping the toy and giving no audience to your message. They will feel obligated to let you tell them your story. You extend your nine second window of opportunity into many minutes, enough to create real interest and gain their favorable audience for as long as it takes. You're familiar with this obligation tactic from charities that send personalized printed return address labels. You see that as a control for so many because it works and it works because it creates obligation and what I call good guilt. The bigger the gift, sometimes called a freemium, the more it obligates. I have for various purposes sent autographed items, personalized coffee mugs, oven mitts tied to a information that's too hot to handle 6 foot long toothbrushes, giant rifle bullets with a rolled up, personalized wanted poster in a tube, toy trucks and bulldozers, stuffed superhero dolls, wind up hamsters that squeal and run about, boxes of animal crackers for a circus themed seminar, plastic safes and piggy banks, and at least a hundred other different items. You've probably seen the little trash cans, often sent with the last step in a multi-step mailing with a note that reads, you've been throwing out my mail so I thought I'd send you a trash can to throw it out in. A client of mine took that up a notch and had a messenger deliver sales letters, printed on big 11 by 17 inch pages in a full-size aluminum trash can. Our joke was, even if the gatekeeper wants to throw it out, what is she going to throw it out in? And of course there is, as Gary described the real dollar bill letter, the two dollar bill letter, up to the real hundred dollar bill letter. I've also used full uncut sheets of two dollar bills. I often use customized million dollar bills with the mailers photo. We've used foreign currency, foreign currency cut in half, coins or custom poker chips in little baggy stapled to the letter. Real money or clever interesting fake money alternatives get attention and can obligate. I and my clients will also often mail with grabbers and objects. So a classic baseball card is stapled to the letter, but there's also a paperweight sized wood baseball bat engraved with the prospect's name in the box. Lumpy envelopes, big packages with something rattling around inside, big fragile and handle with care decals justified by what's inside. This sort of thing gets attention. The million dollar idea is to make what is attached and closed obligating. Let me quickly share a few resources. The most knowledgeable experts and useful vendors I know of in the grabber object and lumpy mail business are Keith and Travis Lee at 3dmailresults.com. They have a huge diversity of grabbers and objects available paired with relevance ideas and supported with examples of successful uses of them and even copy and campaign templates and they can source just about anything. That's 3dmailresults.com. Another terrific one stop shop for object mail campaigns is Direct Mail Concierge, which can be seen at yourdmcshop.com. At a great place to shop on your own, especially if you are looking for dirt cheap prices, is orientaltrading.com. I also go to toy stores and dollar stores for inspiration. Gary mentioned photos. This is a staple of political and charitable fundraising, but foolishly neglected by a lot of for-profit businesses and marketers. Photo images can be licensed from a number of online sources or created and if they are very relevant to a target audience, they serve both my purposes, attention and obligation. A fine photo, for example, of Arlington National Cemetery will not be hastily discarded by military veterans of a certain age. I have a client who sells something that creates enormous instant skepticism to mailing lists of buyers who have been abused and are very skeptical. He nearly tripled his response by adding different sized, full-color photos, including an aerial view of their small town, of a Friday night high school football game, a photo of their office building and a family photo with handwritten notes about each photo on the backs. I have personally used photos of me and my Rolls-Royce convertible, which formerly belonged to Dean Martin, my other classic cars, me with my dog named the Million Dollar Dog, with my horses, with my wife at Disney World. As Gary said, this is a great way to humanize a pitch and to support a story. One of the best things about enclosing a photo, though, is it supports use of one of the all-time best pieces of outside envelope teaser copy ever invented? Yes, I know Gary's position on teaser copy. I can do the A-pile, B-pile wrap verbatim. Should he hear me advocating teaser copy on envelopes, he might return from the Great Beyond to argue. But the problem with dogma is that you might step in it. Most teachers default to dogmatic positions. People want absolutes, 10 commandments, not 16 maybes. But the only 100% truthful response to any direct marketing question is, test. I have beaten perfectly executed A-pile mail with junked up outer envelopes and vice versa. And I and many of my clients do a lot of mailing to established customers about our next new things. We don't need to sneak up on them, but we don't take their readership for granted either. Anyway, the very powerful line of teaser copy is, photos enclosed do not bend. I do want to share a story directly related to Gary's counterweighting of personal photos and letter up against impersonal brochure. This is a story Gary would have great sympathy for. He has been in this place. It's not unique, but it is instructive. Years back, I did a major beat the control project for one of the biggest weight loss companies. They mailed millions of pieces of direct mail about six times a year. I went up against two different established controls and two new tests by their newest Madison Avenue ad agency, Rufus Dufus and Stupid. The piece I had the mail was in a personal stationary size, modeled pink envelope, hand addressed in blue ink, live stamp, no business identity. Inside a 16 page letter printed on the same personal sized pink paper, body copy in black typewriter font, plus blue ink handwritten notes in the margins. It was one long, long testimonial. A first person voiced human interest story, which did not reveal the product, company or offer until page 14. There were also three small photos enclosed, her before picture, her after picture, and a picture of her in a bathing suit water skiing. This mailing whipped all the others by wide margins. The CEO called and said he had good news and bad news. His good news was that it was the hands down winner. The bad news was they would never mail it beyond the just concluded test. To test it, he could do as he pleased. To roll it out, he had to show it to the corporate board of directors, who set themselves on fire and absolutely forbid mailing a piece without brand identity and logo prominent everywhere. A piece that looked like it was done on an antique typewriter for God's sakes. A piece that was just embarrassing. I was saying about companies, the bigger they get, the dumber they get. The pieces I beat were, of course, as Gary described, cold, factual, slick, professional brochures. What's instructive is that big corporations have mixed agendas. Maximum response or even maximum money is rarely the number one governing goal. Other concerns and constituencies interfere, like a board's ego and ignorance. What Wall Street will think and so forth. The advantage of the entrepreneur is that he can laser focus on what works best, period. Finally, I want to say something about serious studentship. A lot of people just aren't real students. Gary was often not seen as a serious person. And like many masters, he made much of what he did seem easy or spontaneous. But he was a very serious student of the profession of direct response marketing and copywriting. He referenced Robert Collier, that's reaching way back to what I call the gold in the old. To direct marketing when you had to get somebody to work to buy. No internet, no 800 numbers, no credit cards. I can usually stump somebody claiming to be a direct marketer before I've named just six such giants that anybody actually engaged in direct marketing should study intently. You decide whether to be an amateur or a pro by who you study and how much you study. I make the case for study ethic every chance I get. If you found me interesting, I can be studied. You can find books at Amazon, book information at nobsbooks.com, newsletter and conference information, including a free trial offer at dankenny.com and resources at dankenny.com forward slash store. Don't email me at any of these sites, by the way, they belong to my publishers, not me. And I do not personally use the internet or email or social media at all, ever. If you have reason to reach out to me personally, contact my assistant Vicki by fax at 602-269-3113. I also want to mention my friend, John Carlton. We talk, we see each other, we get opportunities to be on stage together or work together only occasionally. And every time I wish it was more often. John does not channel or imitate Gary any more than I do. But he has internalized some of the Halbert powers and is both a world-class copywriter and a world-class teacher of copywriting. You have to study Carlton. The next time you get or send a letter that starts out, as you can see, I've attached a whatever to this letter. Why have I done such a strange thing? I have three reasons. Know that our old friend is smiling. Thanks to Bond for inviting me to participate and thanks for listening. You've been listening to an original issue of the Gary Halbert letter. You can get more free, powerful and timeless marketing advice in a mobile friendly format by signing up at thegarryhalbertletter.com. Again, that's thegarryhalbertletter.com. See you there.
